name: Circe
shortDescription: A powerful sorceress who lived on the island of Aeaea, known for her knowledge of potions and herbs, and her ability to transform humans into animals.
description: Circe is a prominent figure in Greek mythology, best known from Homer's *Odyssey*. She is a sorceress who resides on the island of Aeaea. Her parentage varies in different accounts, but she is often described as the daughter of Helios, the sun god, and Perse, an Oceanid nymph, or Hecate. Circe is renowned for her mastery of magic, particularly her skill in using herbs and potions to transform people into animals. Her encounter with Odysseus and his crew is a pivotal episode in the *Odyssey*. When Odysseus' men arrive on Aeaea, Circe invites them to a feast, but she secretly laces the food with a potion that turns them into swine. Odysseus, guided by Hermes, who provides him with the herb moly to protect him from Circe's magic, confronts the sorceress and forces her to restore his men to their human form. Odysseus and his crew remain on Aeaea for a year, during which time Circe becomes Odysseus' lover and provides him with valuable advice for his journey home, including warnings about the Sirens, Scylla, and Charybdis.
subtitle: The Enchantress of Aeaea, Transformer of Men
achievements: Detained Odysseus on her island for a year
Provided Odysseus with crucial guidance for his journey
Mastered the art of herbalism and potion-making
Possessed immense magical power and knowledge
quests: Assisting Odysseus on his journey home
Living on the island of Aeaea

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Circe's origins are rooted in ancient Greek mythology. She is typically described as the daughter of Helios, the sun god, and Perse, an Oceanid nymph. Some accounts also name Hecate as her mother. Her siblings include Pasiphae and Aeetes. Circe's island, Aeaea, is a place of both beauty and danger, shrouded in mystery and filled with exotic creatures. The most famous myth involving Circe is her encounter with Odysseus and his crew in Homer's *Odyssey*. Upon landing on Aeaea, Odysseus sends a scouting party led by Eurylochus. All except Eurylochus are lured into Circe's palace, where she transforms them into pigs. Odysseus, warned by Eurylochus, seeks the help of Hermes, who gives him the herb moly to protect him from Circe's magic. Odysseus confronts Circe, resists her powers, and forces her to restore his men. He then stays with her for a year, enjoying her hospitality and gaining valuable knowledge for his journey.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Circe plays a significant role in the *Odyssey* as both an obstacle and a helper to Odysseus. Initially, she represents a threat to Odysseus and his men, embodying the dangers of temptation and the power of female sorcery. Her ability to transform men into animals highlights the theme of the bestial nature of humans and the potential for degradation. However, after Odysseus subdues her, Circe transforms into a valuable ally. She provides him with essential advice and warnings about the perils that lie ahead, including the Sirens, Scylla, and Charybdis. Her guidance is crucial for Odysseus' successful return to Ithaca. Circe also represents the allure of pleasure and the potential for distraction from one's goals. Odysseus' year-long stay with her symbolizes the temptation to abandon one's responsibilities and succumb to a life of ease and indulgence.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Circe is a complex figure whose symbolism is multifaceted. She is often interpreted as a representation of female power, particularly in the realm of magic and seduction. Her ability to transform men into animals can be seen as a symbol of the power of women to emasculate or control men. Her knowledge of herbs and potions also connects her to the natural world and the ancient traditions of witchcraft. Circe's island, Aeaea, symbolizes a liminal space, a place between worlds where the boundaries between human and animal, reality and illusion, become blurred. The herb moly, given to Odysseus by Hermes, represents divine intervention and the power of reason to overcome the forces of irrationality and temptation. Circe's transformation from a threat to an ally can be interpreted as a symbol of the potential for redemption and the importance of seeking guidance from unexpected sources.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Circe's character has had a lasting influence on literature, art, and popular culture. Her story has been retold and reinterpreted in various forms throughout the centuries. In classical literature, she appears in works by Ovid and other Roman writers. During the Renaissance, Circe was often depicted in paintings and sculptures as a symbol of female power and seduction. In modern literature, Circe has been reimagined in novels such as Madeline Miller's *Circe*, which offers a sympathetic portrayal of the sorceress and explores her complex motivations. Circe continues to be a popular figure in contemporary fantasy and mythology-inspired works. Her name is also associated with the concept of 'circean,' used to describe someone who is alluring and dangerous. The enduring appeal of Circe lies in her enigmatic nature and the enduring fascination with the power of magic and female agency.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: The primary source for Circe's story, detailing her encounter with Odysseus and his men.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: Provides a genealogical account of Circe and her family.

associations.name: Magic
associations.significance: Circe is a powerful sorceress known for her mastery of magic.

associations.name: Swine
associations.significance: She transforms Odysseus' men into swine, a key event in her myth.

associations.name: Seduction
associations.significance: She uses her allure to trap and control those who come to her island.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Baba Yaga
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A powerful, ambiguous female figure living in a remote location, associated with magic and transformation.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Morgan le Fay
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A sorceress with knowledge of healing and magic, sometimes portrayed as an antagonist, sometimes as a helper.

companions.name: Odysseus
companions.description: The hero who encounters Circe and becomes her lover for a year.

companions.name: Helios
companions.description: The sun god, often identified as Circe's father.

companions.name: Hermes
companions.description: The messenger god who aids Odysseus by providing the herb moly.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of herbs and potions
worshipPractices.description: While not widely worshipped, those who sought her favor might offer herbs or potions.

cultural.modernLegacy: Circe continues to inspire artists, writers, and filmmakers, often representing powerful and independent female figures. Her story is reinterpreted in modern novels and films, exploring themes of female agency, magic, and transformation.
